hero.eyebrow: Specialty Representative, Psychiatry · AbbVie · McKinney, TX
hero.name: Kelsey

narrative.title: Why psychiatry, why AbbVie
narrative.body:
I spent the first part of my career in institutional finance, most recently at Goldman Sachs. The real skill there wasn't the numbers. It was earning the trust of clients with limited time, reading what each of them needed, and knowing dense, legal-jargon-filled contracts well enough to solve real problems with them. In 2023, I left to become a full-time parent.

In 2025, I watched a stranger save someone's life during a medical emergency. That moment really impacted me, and it pushed me to earn my EMT license. During my clinicals in the ER, I saw two kinds of psychiatric patients: those with no physical injury who were clearly suffering, and those whose physical emergencies traced back to a psychiatric condition left untreated too long. I had long cared about mental health, but seeing those patients in every ER I rotated through pushed me to be part of the solution.

I've always been achievement-oriented and tenacious about whatever I take on, so when a friend told me my personality was built for pharmaceutical sales, it clicked. The first thing I did was look for a product I could genuinely believe in, and that search led me to AbbVie Psychiatry. My reasons are personal. People I love have struggled with mental illness, and I know the helplessness of watching it. I have seen what the right medicine and the right advocacy can do, and I have seen the cost when neither arrives: loneliness, homelessness, suicide. As a CASA volunteer helping abused and neglected children and their families, I learned how few resources reach the people who need them. That is why I want to be part of this space.

AbbVie has a reputation for patient-centric care, and Vraylar is the jewel of its psychiatry portfolio. After looking into it, I see why. It is a D3-preferring partial agonist, with far higher affinity for the D3 receptor than other atypical antipsychotics, where D3 occupancy is usually negligible. It covers both bipolar and schizophrenia, is a once-a-day easy take, carries a comparatively favorable weight and metabolic profile, and its active metabolite has the longest half-life of any atypical antipsychotic, which gives patients real forgiveness on a missed dose. It has also made a great companion to SSRI and SNRI treatments for depression, which mainly target serotonin, while Vraylar is a partial agonist at serotonin 5-HT1A and dopamine D2 and D3, and an antagonist at 5-HT2A and 5-HT2B. The pipeline behind it is expanding, with bretisilocin, a psychedelic for depression acquired from Gilgamesh, and ABBV-932 for bipolar depression and generalized anxiety out of the long-running Gedeon Richter partnership that produced Vraylar itself. Reading that AbbVie's neuroscience portfolio "generated net revenues of $10.8 billion and is our fastest growing therapeutic area" makes me genuinely excited for the trajectory of this team.

I also know this market. I lived in Prosper the past few years, and McKinney and the DFW suburbs are growing faster than the psychiatric provider base can keep up with, which means every prescriber here is carrying a heavier load. My approach is simple: know my product inside out, build real trusting relationships, listen for what each provider actually needs, and be the solution. I watched one prepared person change a life. I intend to build a career helping clinicians do that every day.

startStories.title: Physician & Stakeholder Relationships
startStories.teaser: Build trusted relationships with prescribers and clinical staff that create real access, from the front desk to the physician.
startStories.body:
At Goldman Sachs, I managed syndicated loan contracts and presented directly to institutional clients and internal stakeholders, where a single misread detail could cost the trust of the relationship. That built real executive presence: the ability to walk into a room, anticipate a decision maker's concerns, and hold their trust through a complex process.

A psychiatric practice runs on the same principle at a different scale. The office manager, the nurse practitioner, and the physician all need something different from a rep who respects their time. I bring the same discipline I used with institutional clients to earning access with a prescriber: show up prepared, know the science cold, and never waste a visit.

startStories.title: Territory & Call Planning
startStories.teaser: Build a call plan grounded in real research, then adapt it as the territory tells you what's actually working.
startStories.body:
During an 18 month volunteer representative role across Nebraska, Iowa, and South Dakota, my results were defined entirely by how many people I could convert into members of our organization. I inherited an underperforming territory and turned it into one of the strongest through territory research, stakeholder mapping, active listening for the real need, and an adaptive approach whenever the obvious path didn't work, a skill set that comes directly from my Gallup Strategic strength.

That same instinct is what I'd bring to a McKinney and North Texas psychiatry territory: research the provider landscape, map who influences the prescribing decision, build a call plan around real coverage and frequency, and keep adjusting it based on what each account is telling me.

startStories.title: Preparation, Then Decisive Action
startStories.teaser: Prepare thoroughly before every call, then act with urgency once the opportunity is in front of you.
startStories.body:
As an EMT finishing in the top 5% of my class, I trained in emergency rooms alongside physicians. During one rotation, a doctor gave rapid instructions in the middle of an emergency surgery situation. With the nurses occupied, he told the three EMT students watching from the corner of the room to jump in and help. In a moment with zero margin for error, I was the only one who moved.

That's my Activator strength in practice: acting the instant the need is clear, but only because the preparation was already done. I bring that same instinct to a sales cycle: build the pre-call plan, know the objections before they come up, and close every conversation with a clear, logical next step instead of letting it drift.

experience.title: Licensed Emergency Medical Technician
experience.body: Top 5% of cohort, delivering hands-on clinical support across the emergency room, trauma bay, ambulance, and in-transit care.
bullets: Provided direct patient care within the emergency department's dedicated psychiatric section and completed training in psychiatric emergency response and pharmacology fundamentals.
Assisted an ER physician during a high-acuity emergency tracheotomy, maintaining composure and executing rapid-response support protocols under extreme pressure.
Supported emergency air transport intake, stabilizing and transferring critical patients from rooftop helipad arrival through trauma bay admission.
Facilitated patient stabilization, transport, and critical care handoffs working alongside physicians, nurses, EMTs, firefighters, and trauma teams.

experience.title: Global Markets Analyst — Client Relationships & Syndicated Loan Operations
experience.body: Managed 20+ institutional relationships across multi-million dollar syndicated loan portfolios, completing 10 to 20 complex transactions weekly with zero-defect accuracy in a 70+ hour workweek.
bullets: Independently identified a contract misinterpretation, resolved the resulting client objection, and aligned internal leadership around the fix, strengthening retention with a senior stakeholder.
Served as primary relationship owner to senior financial decision-makers, executing white-glove service standards to differentiate the firm's value proposition.

experience.title: Business Operations Analyst
experience.body: Diagnosed territory-level performance gaps and built executive forecasting models in SQL, Snowflake, and Python to translate complex data into clear, board-aligned growth strategy.
bullets: Presented a C-suite business case that secured a $2M+ revenue expansion opportunity and directly influenced executive resource allocation.

experience.title: Financial Analyst Intern
experience.body: Built a nationwide marketing performance heat map across 42,000+ U.S. ZIP codes to identify underperforming spend, market segments, and high-potential growth territories.
bullets: Trained the Analytic Team on the newly developed targeting framework.

experience.title: Executive Director (Volunteer) — BYU Chapter
experience.body: Presided over a 5-person leadership team overseeing 100+ volunteer advocates supporting court-involved children in a high-stakes judicial environment. Many of the children I advocated for were navigating serious mental health needs, which gave me an early, first-hand understanding of what's at stake in psychiatric care.
bullets: Built trusted relationships with judges, attorneys, families, and multidisciplinary stakeholders through emotionally intelligent communication.

experience.title: Full-Time Volunteer Representative — Nebraska, Iowa & South Dakota
experience.body: Grew membership across a 3-state territory by identifying and developing influential community advocates who referred and influenced others, reducing reliance on cold outreach.
bullets: Created pre-call plan objectives and executed post-call evaluations, tracked through a CRM pipeline, to continuously improve outreach effectiveness.
Onboarded and mentored a new representative while consistently meeting daily and weekly performance targets.
